speaker: interviewer
text: Today is February 26, 1995. This is an interview with Lillian Eckstein. I'm Amy Friedman, the interviewer. And it's taking place in New York, in the United States of America, in English. And this is tape number two. OK, Mrs. Eckstein, we were talking about going to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto-- life in ghetto was horrible. People were-- were murdered. They did not know what to believe, what to expect. The younger people had no idea, but the older ones felt that there was some program-- pogrom-- waiting for them. They did not believe the propaganda from the Germans, that they're taking them to work. Because they congregated the children, they did not take the people who were capable of working.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the older people were very suspicious. But they did not know what to do. They didn't have the guts to fight back like they did in the Warsaw ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The people in Czechoslovakia, they were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] intelligent, obedient. They-- they did not know how to be aggressive. And so we did what we were told to do. We were lead-- led by, like a kettle into the fire. Well, today we know better. We were not-- I hope that something like this will never happen again. Because by now, we know what some people are capable of, which is-- which is beyond human nature-- that what the intelligentsia, the so-called elite of Europe, the generals, the politicians, can be as vicious, as cruel as murderers, by taking small babies. They did not commit any crime. They had no time to do anything wrong in life. And they were just butchering them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what the ghetto looked like, the physical?

speaker: interviewee
text: People, as I mentioned to you before, they were bewildered, people walking up and down, trying to figure out what to do. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was no way out, because we were surrounded with German Gestapo, with tanks, weapons, and Hungarian SS. So we didn't-- we just-- we were powerless.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it physically look like? Where were you physically?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a-- a barracks, barracks. They had no heat, no facilities of preparing food, or-- or cleanliness. It's just a chaos, is a complete chaos. We were just doing what we were told to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with your family in your own space?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, a very small space. And some babies were crying, and older people were dying. And the misery was unbearable.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much did you eat? What did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were giving us a soup, once a day, with a slice of bread. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It wasn't so much-- I did not even feel the hunger anymore, because apparently I got so used to living without food. But the worst part of it was my younger brothers. They-- [SIGHS] they couldn't-- they couldn't take it. And the babies couldn't take it. Ugh, my back is killing me. Excuse me, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And so we were waiting to be transported to wherever we were told to we should go. And we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who in your family was there? Where were the different members of your family at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in different barracks. Everybody had a cubicle, a little place where they-- where they stayed together. And they just went-- they picked out a barrack. And that particular barrack was loaded onto the cattle trains. And they were taken off to Germany. And we did not know where that cattle train was going. But soon after a few days of being on that cattle train, with the children and old people, and there was no toilet facilities, and there was no food and no water. And the cries, and the pain was-- and the odor was unbearable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Back to the ghetto for a moment, where were-- was your family together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were together. And we-- we were taken into the cattle trains together. And my grandmother died in the train. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were just loading them up in the corner, and we were sitting in top of them until we arrived to Auschwitz-Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was that train ride?

speaker: interviewee
text: Few days. I don't know the days. I didn't know-- I lost track of the days and of the nights. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When we arrived to Birkenau, Auschwitz, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they opened up the trains. And they started screaming [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] to get off the train in German. We saw men in striped clothing, and-- and the German Gestapo with the tanks, and with weapons, surrounding us. And we were marching towards the front, all of us. And when we approached a certain man, which I later found out was Dr. Mengele-- Dr. Josef Mengele-- he had white gloves. He was a tall, attractive looking man. And he would just look at you, and he would point with the finger, right or left. My mother [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] was ordered to go to the left-- to the right. And I was going to the left. We were facing him, to the left. And my mother took my sister's little children on her hands, and they left. They-- that was the last time I saw them. And my sister, myself, we were going to the left. And they took us into a-- a barrack. They shaved off our heads. And they told us, before we entered, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you do what you're told to do-- if you do not, you will go out through the chimney. And we looked up, and we saw a smell, a-- a smelly odor. And the smoke was coming out. We didn't know. So all they told us to keep quiet and do what we're told to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took out clothes off, they shaved our hair, and we were put in the shower. And, in the nude, they told us to stay [NON-ENGLISH], five people in a row.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was the beginning. We took us-- they took us to the [NON-ENGLISH] in Birkenau. As a matter of fact, this summer, I saw the bunk that I was in-- in the [NON-ENGLISH], and the bunk number was number 13. I even found the bed that we were in, six girls in one small space. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And that was the life in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they led you to the bunker the first day? Who was there? Who was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people who came without transport. They were all lead into that bunk, bunk number 13. And-- and that's where we stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was life like, at that point, for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every morning, around-- we-- we never knew the time, nobody had a watch. So we only knew when the day came and when the night came. Early in the morning, then the-- when it started getting light, they took-- they marched us out from the bunk. And we had to stay [NON-ENGLISH], five people in a row. And counting-- they were counting us-- how many of them died, how many are still alive? And so we stayed there sometimes for hours in the cold. And then they chased us back into the bunk, and they gave a bowl of soup. And we stayed there waiting. We were waiting there to be transported into Germany to-- for work. We assumed that that's what the train-- that this is a transient camp. So we waited. I don't know how much time I spent there. I had lost track of time. One day, they took half of our bunk, and half of the other bunk. And they transported us to Oberschlesien.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Eckstein, can we-- I want to get to that, but I just want to go back. What was your food and clothing situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we received the striped clothing, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and their wooden shoes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that was our clothes-- clothing. And we-- the food was a bowl of soup once a day, and a slice of bread. That was our meal for the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you were separated from your mother and your nieces?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sisters and brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the rest of your family at that point in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: (SIGHING) I don't know. I don't know where they were. They just went to the other side. And that's the last-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [CRYING] Excuse me. That was the last time I saw them. [CRYING] Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're doing great.

speaker: interviewee
text: The worst part of it is that they died a horrible life, a horrible death. Because when they took them into the gas chamber, they were suffocating. [CRYING] They were suffocating [SOBBING] before they died. And that's a horrible death. And my mother didn't deserve this, neither did my brothers and sisters. [SOBBING] I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. Don't be sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you want to kill somebody, just do it. Don't torture them. Don't torture them. Because there is a barbaric behavior of torturing babies who are grasping for air.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- I went in there this summer, into that gas chamber. It's bombed. They bombed it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And these ashes-- the ashes are still there. I brought pieces from Auschwitz. May I show them to you? Why don't we get to that in the next part. Would that be OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with any of your sisters, once you went to the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was. I was with my sister who is here. And she is unable to be here because she's not well. [SIGHS] I was with another sister. We went to Oberschlesien. We worked there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we stay in Auschwitz? With your sisters, were they in that same bunk with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do to pass the time? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were just-- I think that, at that time, we were very disturbed, very emotional about the situation. Because we didn't know what to-- what to expect. Are we going to wind up in the gas chamber, too? Because later on, we found out from the kapos, the people who worked there, but they were also-- they were also Jews-- secretly they told us that when we see the four corners of Birkenau is gas chambers. That's where they burn the people. And they told us if-- eventually that we may wind up there, too. That's where our parents are, and that's where we going to wind up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you are being told that you're going to wind up in a gas chamber, what-- what any normal person can think? But the mean time, if you-- if they want to do away with you, do it. Don't wait to suffer. Not to know what the next day will bring. So, may I just tell you what happened later on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, absolutely. [SIGHS] Well, they-- one morning, they came out, and they told us to come out to [NON-ENGLISH]. And they told us that we are going to be going in a train to work, to Germany. And so they-- they picked out 2,000 people-- 2,000 girls. And they took us to Oberschlesien, where we worked in Schutzengraben. It's ditches-- where you make ditches. So when the Russian army was coming, the tanks would fall in there, and they wouldn't be able to get out. And that's where they put us to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea how long you were in Auschwitz before Oberschlesien?

speaker: interviewee
text: Few months. I don't know the time and the date. I don't remember dates anymore. I don't-- I never talk about it. I don't-- I buried it. And-- and I don't know the dates.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if it was winter or summer when you went from Auschwitz to--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in the late winter, it must have been in April or March, sometime that day. But it was very cold there, because it's very close to the Soviet Union. And-- and we did hear a lot of shooting going on in that area, bombing. But we didn't know if it's the Germans or-- or-- we didn't know that the Russians were approaching. So after a few months--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they get you to Oberschlesien?

speaker: interviewee
text: By-- by cattle trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was-- can you bring us through that? What was that like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same-- they throw you into the cattle train and they take you closer to Polish-German borders. And, by now, I realized that the Russians were approaching. And the bombing and the shooting that went on was from the partisans. The partisans were surrounded in the mountain area. And they knew that-- that they're burning people in Auschwitz. They tried to get messages to us through the underground-- the partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they succeed?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of them-- one of them was caught and brutally murdered right in front of us. He was trying to deliver a message from the mountain area, that we should try to escape or break out. But who had the energy? And it was very cold there. And we-- we were not human anymore, by that time. We were just doing what we were told to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: So while we were digging these ditches, probably half of the girls died there because of the cold, starvation, beating. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we dig the ditches-- when they digged the ditches, they put green stuff on top, so when the tanks came, they didn't realize that it's a-- a grave there. And when the Russians were approaching, we heard planes. And they were trying to throw leaflets down. But nobody would dare bend down to pick anything up. One morning, they told us, we're leaving. So they put us on--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we stay in Oberschlesien just for a moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you living? How were you living at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were living in-- it was in stables for cows. Because they were not prepared to have [GERMAN]-- I mean, [GERMAN], it's in German. They didn't prepared to have people there. So they didn't-- they didn't build homes for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was where they kept the cows, I believe. Because it was a lot of straw and all of that stuff going.

speaker: interviewee
text: The German population-- when we walked through to work in the towns-- they were looking, staring. And they were afraid to say anything, or some of them tried to throw some food when we were marching. They saw that we wore the striped clothing. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And the life there was quite miserable, because people who couldn't work anymore, they were being beaten up or shoot. They were collapsing. And so when we left Oberschlesien--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we stay there just for one, a little bit more time? Do you know what town you would walk through?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were little towns around-- surrounded. It was on the Polish-German border. But we realized that there was something going on because of the shooting and the planes. So we were even afraid to look up into the sky, because we were watched. I mean, we were afraid for our own shadow. By that time, we were not human anymore. By that time, we couldn't think. We couldn't apprehend the situation, because we were like zombies-- they would call it in this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they--how did the Germans achieve that? What were things--

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe that, now that my daughter is a doctor, and I read a lot about how they managed to brainwash people and how they managed to numb somebody's mind, or cripple your mind-- in Auschwitz, in the food, they put in bromide. This friend of mine who worked in the kitchen said that every barrel of soup, they used to put in five kilograms of bromide-- which prevents people from-- it's like a tranquilizer. It stopped women from menstruating. It-- your mind gets numb. I'm not a doctor, but from what I-- I hear and read and understand that, they used some kind of a method to just take away your human-- that you're a human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: This friend who worked in the kitchen, do you do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's here. She's here. She had-- she had two children. She's a-- she is a schoolteacher here. And she's extremely-- she's suffering from malignant-- malignant cancer. I-- I would like you to interview her if possible, if she's able to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do her know name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Her name is Ms. Adler-- Beszy Adler.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when she shared with you the bromide story--

speaker: interviewee
text: She, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she know that that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was more-- she was already an older-- older girl. And she realized that when they cooked that-- the soup, was-- which was made out of [GERMAN]-- IT was a [GERMAN]. Oh, you don't speak German. It's a powder, a powder. And they put the bromide in the soup. I mean, she just read what they are. It was no instructions, but it said bromide.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she saw them put that in the soup?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the soup. Either then they did, or whatever other methods they used, I have no idea. But all I know is, looking back, that how they can dehumanize a human being-- a person-- and not being able to say something, and do-- do what they want you to do. Not because you committed a crime, or you are guilty of any crime, but just so because you happen to be born by a Jewish family. This was your crime. And as you see, we are clean, we are fairly intelligent people. My mother--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just want to warn you, the tape is about to end, so let's stop here and we'll continue right there.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
